content.content:
1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides two independent container image capabilities: 4 5 - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce, fetch, retain, and serve images using a role-managed private cloud registry — even can coordinate egress-locked builds with full supply-chain provenance 6 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — even using images unmodified from "in the wild" 7 8 > [!IMPORTANT] 9 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** 10 > 11 > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the SLSA attestation chain, build isolation, and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 12 > 13 > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. 14 > 15 > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 16 17 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is a set of bash scripts designed for incorporation into arbitrary projects. 18 The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. 19 After initial manual setup, all cloud API calls use `openssl` + `curl`. 20 A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. 21 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. 22 23 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 24 25

26 27

28 29 ## Environment 30 31 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable provenance, and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. 32 The two compose but neither requires the other. 33 34 All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. 35 Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). 36 Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). 37 38 Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. 39 The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. 40 Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands. 41 Each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) is named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece tells you what it does. 42 43 Every state-changing [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) writes three [Log](#Log) files to the directory named by `BURS_LOG_DIR` in your [BURS](#BURS) station file: a stable-name file (always the same path — easy for tooling to locate and evaluate the most recent run), a per-command file (same command name across runs — tools like SlickEdit sense diffs between executions), and a timestamped historical file (permanent record). 44 Disk space is cheap; [Log](#Log) unconditionally so the diagnostic evidence is always there when something fails. 45 Handbooks don't [Log](#Log) — teaching output is ephemeral. 46 47 A [Transcript](#Transcript) is a single file capturing key decision points and state transitions within a [Tabtarget's](#Tabtarget) execution. 48 Where [Logs](#Log) preserve full terminal output, a [Transcript](#Transcript) records the structured progress of sophisticated orchestration commands — the first thing to read when debugging a multi-step failure. 49 50 The [Output](#Output) directory is a fixed-path staging area cleared and recreated before each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) runs. Commands that produce artifacts write them here. Concurrent bash sessions share this path, so parallel commands can overwrite each other's [Output](#Output). 51 52 To begin, run the onboarding walkthrough: 53 54 ``` 55 tt/rbw-o.OnboardingStartHere.sh 56 ``` 57 58 ## Foundry 59 60 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. 61 The [Foundry](#Foundry) encompasses [Depots](#Depot), [Vessels](#Vessel), [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. 62 Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) (egress-locked build from source with SLSA provenance), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). 63 Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. 64 65 The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. 66 Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned [Depot](#Depot) registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. 67 68 ### Depot 69 70 The facility where container images are built and stored — a GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket. 71 The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. 72 Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. 73 74 Each [Depot](#Depot) operates in one of two build egress profiles: 75 76 - **[Tethered](#Tethered)** — Build egress mode allowing public internet access during Cloud Build. [Tethered](#Tethered) builds pull base images from upstream registries at build time — simpler to set up, but dependent on upstream availability. Compare with [Airgap](#Airgap). 77 - **[Airgap](#Airgap)** — Build egress mode with no public internet access during Cloud Build. [Airgap](#Airgap) builds draw all dependencies from [Enshrined](#Enshrine) images in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — fully self-contained, independent of upstream availability. Requires [Enshrining](#Enshrine) base images before the first build. Compare with [Tethered](#Tethered). 78 79 ### Payor 80 81 Owns the GCP project and funds it; authenticates via OAuth. 82 The [Payor](#Payor) is the only role requiring manual Google Cloud Console interaction — [Establishing](#Establish) the project, configuring OAuth, and [Installing](#Install) credentials via browser flow. 83 All other roles descend from credentials the [Payor's](#Payor) infrastructure creates. 84 85 ### Governor 86 87 Administers a [Depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access. 88 The [Governor](#Governor) is [Mantled](#Mantle) by the [Payor](#Payor) and holds the administrative credential for the [Depot](#Depot). 89 The [Governor](#Governor) [Knights](#Knight) [Directors](#Director) for build access, [Charters](#Charter) [Retrievers](#Retriever) for pull access, and [Forfeits](#Forfeit) credentials when they are no longer needed. 90 91 ### Director 92 93 Builds and publishes [Vessel](#Vessel) images into a [Depot](#Depot). 94 Each [Director](#Director) credential is scoped to one [Depot](#Depot). 95 The [Director](#Director) manages the image lifecycle: [Ordain](#Ordain) a build, [Tally](#Tally) registry health, [Vouch](#Vouch) provenance, [Abjure](#Abjure) superseded artifacts, and [Jettison](#Jettison) individual tags. 96 97 ### Retriever 98 99 Pulls and runs [Vessel](#Vessel) images from a [Depot](#Depot). 100 This is the most constrained role — read-only access to the [Depot](#Depot) registry.

# Recipe Bottle [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides two independent container image capabilities: - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce, fetch, retain, and serve images using a role-managed private cloud registry — even can coordinate egress-locked builds with full supply-chain provenance - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — even using images unmodified from "in the wild" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** > > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the SLSA attestation chain, build isolation, and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. > > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. > > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is a set of bash scripts designed for incorporation into arbitrary projects. The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. After initial manual setup, all cloud API calls use `openssl` + `curl`. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle

## Environment [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable provenance, and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. The two compose but neither requires the other. All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands. Each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) is named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece tells you what it does. Every state-changing [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) writes three [Log](#Log) files to the directory named by `BURS_LOG_DIR` in your [BURS](#BURS) station file: a stable-name file (always the same path — easy for tooling to locate and evaluate the most recent run), a per-command file (same command name across runs — tools like SlickEdit sense diffs between executions), and a timestamped historical file (permanent record). Disk space is cheap; [Log](#Log) unconditionally so the diagnostic evidence is always there when something fails. Handbooks don't [Log](#Log) — teaching output is ephemeral. A [Transcript](#Transcript) is a single file capturing key decision points and state transitions within a [Tabtarget's](#Tabtarget) execution. Where [Logs](#Log) preserve full terminal output, a [Transcript](#Transcript) records the structured progress of sophisticated orchestration commands — the first thing to read when debugging a multi-step failure. The [Output](#Output) directory is a fixed-path staging area cleared and recreated before each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) runs. Commands that produce artifacts write them here. Concurrent bash sessions share this path, so parallel commands can overwrite each other's [Output](#Output). To begin, run the onboarding walkthrough: ``` tt/rbw-o.OnboardingStartHere.sh ``` ## Foundry [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. The [Foundry](#Foundry) encompasses [Depots](#Depot), [Vessels](#Vessel), [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) (egress-locked build from source with SLSA provenance), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned [Depot](#Depot) registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. ### Depot The facility where container images are built and stored — a GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket. The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. Each [Depot](#Depot) operates in one of two build egress profiles: - **[Tethered](#Tethered)** — Build egress mode allowing public internet access during Cloud Build. [Tethered](#Tethered) builds pull base images from upstream registries at build time — simpler to set up, but dependent on upstream availability. Compare with [Airgap](#Airgap). - **[Airgap](#Airgap)** — Build egress mode with no public internet access during Cloud Build. [Airgap](#Airgap) builds draw all dependencies from [Enshrined](#Enshrine) images in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — fully self-contained, independent of upstream availability. Requires [Enshrining](#Enshrine) base images before the first build. Compare with [Tethered](#Tethered). ### Payor Owns the GCP project and funds it; authenticates via OAuth. The [Payor](#Payor) is the only role requiring manual Google Cloud Console interaction — [Establishing](#Establish) the project, configuring OAuth, and [Installing](#Install) credentials via browser flow. All other roles descend from credentials the [Payor's](#Payor) infrastructure creates. ### Governor Administers a [Depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access. The [Governor](#Governor) is [Mantled](#Mantle) by the [Payor](#Payor) and holds the administrative credential for the [Depot](#Depot). The [Governor](#Governor) [Knights](#Knight) [Directors](#Director) for build access, [Charters](#Charter) [Retrievers](#Retriever) for pull access, and [Forfeits](#Forfeit) credentials when they are no longer needed. ### Director Builds and publishes [Vessel](#Vessel) images into a [Depot](#Depot). Each [Director](#Director) credential is scoped to one [Depot](#Depot). The [Director](#Director) manages the image lifecycle: [Ordain](#Ordain) a build, [Tally](#Tally) registry health, [Vouch](#Vouch) provenance, [Abjure](#Abjure) superseded artifacts, and [Jettison](#Jettison) individual tags. ### Retriever Pulls and runs [Vessel](#Vessel) images from a [Depot](#Depot). This is the most constrained role — read-only access to the [Depot](#Depot) registry.

content.content: 100 This is the most constrained role — read-only access to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. 101 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use, [Plumbs](#Plumb) their provenance, or [Wrests](#Wrest) a specific image directly. 102 103 ### Vessel 104 105 A specification for a container image — built from source ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrored from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushed from local ([Graft](#Graft)). 106 Each [Vessel](#Vessel) is a directory under `rbev-vessels/` containing at minimum an `rbrv.env` configuration file; [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) also include a Dockerfile. 107 A fourth mode, [Kludge](#Kludge), builds locally for development without involving the [Depot](#Depot). 108 109 ### Hallmark 110 111 A specific build instance of a [Vessel](#Vessel), identified by timestamp. 112 [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) are the unit of provenance tracking — each one records when and how the image was produced. 113 Each [Hallmark](#Hallmark) produces three tagged artifacts in the [Depot](#Depot) registry: the container image (`-image`), the software bill of materials ([`-about`](#About)), and the cryptographic attestation ([`-vouch`](#Vouch)). 114 [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) files to pin a [Crucible](#Crucible) to specific image versions. 115 116 ### Foundry Lifecycle 117 118 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) uses a role-based security model with four roles, each building on the previous: 119 120 | Role | Authenticates via | Purpose | 121 |------|-------------------|---------| 122 | [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [Governor](#Governor) lifecycle | 123 | [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [Depot](#Depot) | 124 | [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies provenance | 125 | [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use | 126 127 The [Payor](#Payor) stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. 128 All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation. 129 130 #### Establishment and Provisioning 131 132 The [Payor](#Payor) begins by [Establishing](#Establish) a GCP project and configuring an OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. 133 After downloading the OAuth client credentials, the [Payor](#Payor) [Installs](#Install) them via a browser authorization flow — the resulting refresh token is stored locally with restrictive permissions and does not need to be repeated. 134 135 With [Payor](#Payor) credentials in place, the [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), provisioning it with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets storage. 136 The [Payor](#Payor) then [Mantles](#Mantle) a [Governor](#Governor) service account to administer the [Depot](#Depot). 137 138 Before the first build can run, the [Depot](#Depot) needs its supply-chain infrastructure in place: upstream base images must be [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the registry, and a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) of builder tool images must be inscribed. 139 140 #### Credential Distribution 141 142 The [Governor](#Governor) creates downstream credentials: [Knighting](#Knight) a [Director](#Director) for build operations and [Chartering](#Charter) a [Retriever](#Retriever) for image pull access. 143 Each credential is scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot). 144 145 #### Build and Retrieve 146 147 The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for each [Vessel](#Vessel) — [Conjuring](#Conjure) from source, [Binding](#Bind) from upstream, or [Grafting](#Graft) from local builds. 148 After builds complete, the [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by health status and [Vouches](#Vouch) their provenance. 149 [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values from the [Tally](#Tally) are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) files, completing the chain from build to runtime. 150 151 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images locally for use. 152 153 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) builds container images on Google Cloud Build (GCB) and stores them in Google Artifact Registry (GAR): 154 155 - Isolated build environments using Google-curated Cloud Build builder images 156 - Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation 157 - SLSA provenance attestation and verification 158 - Software Bills of Material (SBOM) for every build 159 - Full build transcripts captured as auxiliary metadata artifacts 160 - Upstream base images [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry, so builds do not depend on third-party registry availability at build time 161 - `gcloud` never runs on the workstation — REST calls via `curl` and `jq` drive all remote operations, and the Google-supplied `gcloud` binary is confined to Cloud Build step containers on the server side 162 163 Each build's source context is packaged as a [Pouch](#Pouch) — the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 164 165 ## Crucible 166 167 The distinctive case [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain provenance. 168 Containers excel at packaging known applications, but running unvetted code poses security risks that ordinary container deployment does not solve. 169 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) assembles a [Crucible](#Crucible) — three cooperating containers where a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces network policy — without requiring modifications to existing container images. 170 The [Bottle](#Bottle) container runs unmodified, in a network namespace prepared by a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle), with all egress flowing through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. 171 172 The [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad running together as one unit for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 173 The [Crucible](#Crucible) is the local safety orchestration — the apparatus that makes running untrusted code practical. 174 [Charging](#Charge) starts all three containers; [Quenching](#Quench) stops and cleans them up. 175 176 ### Nameplate 177 178 Per-[Vessel](#Vessel) configuration tying a [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). 179 The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations. 180 Each [Nameplate](#Nameplate) declares its [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) pins, and the network policy that the [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces. 181 182 ### Containers 183 184 - **[Sentry](#Sentry)** — Security container enforcing network policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq`. The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: DNS-level filtering (only allowed domain names resolve) and IP-level filtering (only allowed CIDR ranges pass). A compromised [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass either layer — the [Sentry](#Sentry) is the sole gateway between the [Bottle](#Bottle) and the outside network. 185 - **[Pentacle](#Pentacle)** — Privileged container establishing the network namespace shared with the [Bottle](#Bottle). The [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runs briefly with elevated privileges to create the network topology, then remains as the namespace anchor. Security policies are enforced from the first packet because the [Sentry](#Sentry) configures the namespace before the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts. 186 - **[Bottle](#Bottle)** — Your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment. The [Bottle](#Bottle) has no direct network access — all traffic routes through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway in a namespace prepared by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle). Any existing container image can run as a [Bottle](#Bottle) without modification. 187 188 ### Crucible Lifecycle 189 190 [Charge](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) to start the [Sentry](#Sentry), [Pentacle](#Pentacle), and [Bottle](#Bottle) together — the [Bottle](#Bottle) is ready for interactive use immediately. 191 [Rack](#Rack) the [Bottle](#Bottle) to shell in, [Hail](#Hail) the [Sentry](#Sentry) to inspect the gateway, or [Scry](#Scry) the network to observe traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. 192 When finished, [Quench](#Quench) the [Crucible](#Crucible) to stop and clean up all three containers. 193 To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its provenance — the full view shows the SBOM, build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. 194 195 ## Appendix: Foundry Operations 196 197 Formal definitions for all [Foundry](#Foundry) operations, organized by lifecycle phase. 198 199 ### Infrastructure

The [Bottle](#Bottle) has no direct network access — all traffic routes through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway in a namespace prepared by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle). content.content: 200 201 **[Establish](#Establish)** — Guided setup of a new GCP project and OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. 202 [Establishing](#Establish) walks the [Payor](#Payor) through project creation, API enablement, and consent screen configuration — the manual prerequisites before any automated operations can run. 203 204 **[Install](#Install)** — Ingest OAuth client credentials from a downloaded JSON key file. 205 [Installing](#Install) triggers a browser authorization flow and stores the resulting refresh token locally with restrictive permissions (`600`). 206 This is a one-time [Payor](#Payor) operation — the refresh token persists until explicitly revoked. 207 208 **[Levy](#Levy)** — Provision a new [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure. 209 [Levying](#Levy) creates the GCP project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and build configuration. 210 This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that binds [Regime](#Regime) configuration to real cloud resources. 211 212 **[Unmake](#Unmake)** — Permanently destroy a [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure — project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and all contents. 213 [Unmaking](#Unmake) is the reverse of [Levying](#Levy). 214 This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation and is irreversible. 215 216 **[Refresh](#Refresh)** — Refresh an expired [Payor](#Payor) OAuth token. 217 OAuth tokens expire periodically; [Refreshing](#Refresh) re-authenticates via the stored refresh token without repeating the full [Install](#Install) flow. 218 Run when [Payor](#Payor) operations fail with authentication errors. 219 220 **[Quota](#Quota)** — Review Cloud Build capacity and usage. 221 [Quota](#Quota) displays the current build minute allocation, consumption, and any throttling in effect for the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project. 222 223 ### Credentials 224 225 **[Mantle](#Mantle)** — Create or replace the [Governor](#Governor) service account for a [Depot](#Depot). 226 [Mantling](#Mantle) is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that provisions the administrative credential — the [Governor](#Governor) inherits the [Payor's](#Payor) authority to manage the [Depot](#Depot) but authenticates via service account key rather than OAuth. 227 228 **[Knight](#Knight)** — Create a [Director](#Director) service account. 229 [Knighting](#Knight) provisions a new credential scoped to build and publish access within a single [Depot](#Depot). 230 231 **[Charter](#Charter)** — Create a [Retriever](#Retriever) service account. 232 [Chartering](#Charter) provisions a new credential scoped to image pull access within a single [Depot](#Depot). 233 234 **[Forfeit](#Forfeit)** — Revoke a service account credential. 235 [Forfeiting](#Forfeit) deletes the service account and its key material from the [Depot](#Depot) — the credential becomes permanently unusable. 236 This is a [Governor](#Governor) operation used when a credential is compromised, no longer needed, or being rotated. 237 238 **[List Service Accounts](#ListSAs)** — Inventory all service accounts issued within a [Depot](#Depot). 239 Shows [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials with their creation dates and status. 240 241 ### Supply Chain 242 243 **[Enshrine](#Enshrine)** — Mirror upstream base images into your [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 244 [Enshrining](#Enshrine) ensures the build pipeline has a fixed, self-contained supply chain — builds draw from project-owned copies rather than depending on third-party registry availability at build time. 245 246 **[Reliquary](#Reliquary)** — Co-versioned set of builder tool images (skopeo, docker, gcloud, syft) inscribed from upstream into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 247 Cloud Build jobs use [Reliquary](#Reliquary) images as step containers, ensuring builds run with known, project-owned toolchains rather than pulling tools from upstream at build time. 248 The [Director](#Director) inscribes a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) before any [Ordain](#Ordain) or [Enshrine](#Enshrine) operation can run. 249 250 ### Building 251 252 **[Ordain](#Ordain)** — Create a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) with full attestation — the production build operation. 253 [Ordaining](#Ordain) is mode-aware: it [Conjures](#Conjure), [Binds](#Bind), or [Grafts](#Graft) depending on the [Vessel's](#Vessel) configuration. 254 Each [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an image in the [Depot](#Depot) registry with associated provenance metadata. 255 256 **[Conjure](#Conjure)** — Cloud Build creates the image from source. 257 [Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an egress-locked environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full SLSA attestation and SBOMs. 258 This is the highest-trust build mode. 259 260 **[Bind](#Bind)** — Mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. 261 [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 262 Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build provenance. 263 264 **[Graft](#Graft)** — Push a locally-built image to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. 265 [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though [About](#About) and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. 266 This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). 267 268 **[Kludge](#Kludge)** — Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without [Depot](#Depot) registry push. 269 [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). 270 The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. 271 272 **[Pouch](#Pouch)** — Build context packaged as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image and pushed to the [Depot's](#Depot) registry before a Cloud Build job runs. 273 The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. 274 This is the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 275 276 ### Verification 277 278 **[Tally](#Tally)** — Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the [Depot](#Depot) registry by health status. 279 [Tallying](#Tally) shows which builds succeeded, which are pending, and which failed. 280 The [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) before [Vouching](#Vouch) to confirm build completion. 281 282 **[Vouch](#Vouch)** — Cryptographic attestation proving a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) was built by trusted infrastructure. 283 The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full SLSA provenance verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no provenance chain. 284 The [Director](#Director) [Vouches](#Vouch) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) after [Tallying](#Tally) their build status. 285 286 **[About](#About)** — Build metadata and software bill of materials for a [Hallmark](#Hallmark). 287 The [About](#About) artifact (`-about` tag) contains the SBOM, build transcript, build configuration snapshot, and key package summaries — bundled as a compressed archive and stored as a Generic Artifact in GAR. 288 Every [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an [About](#About) alongside the image. 289 290 **[Plumb](#Plumb)** — Inspect an artifact's provenance — SBOM, build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain. 291 [Plumbing](#Plumb) provides full transparency into how an image was built and what it contains. 292 Two views are available: full (SBOM, build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). 293 294 ### Distribution 295 296 **[Summon](#Summon)** — Pull a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [Depot](#Depot) to your local machine. 297 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use — the final step before a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) can be used in a [Crucible](#Crucible). 298 299 **[Wrest](#Wrest)** — Pull a specific image from the [Depot](#Depot) registry by reference. 300 [Wresting](#Wrest) is a direct pull without [Vouch](#Vouch) verification — used when you need a specific image tag regardless of attestation status. 301 Compare with [Summon](#Summon), which enforces the [Vouch](#Vouch) ceremony. 302 303 ### Removal 304 305 **[Abjure](#Abjure)** — Remove a [Hallmark's](#Hallmark) artifacts from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — the `-image`, [`-about`](#About), and [`-vouch`](#Vouch) tags deleted as a coherent unit. 306 [Abjuring](#Abjure) is the reverse of [Ordaining](#Ordain): it formally renounces a build instance. 307 The [Director](#Director) [Abjures](#Abjure) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) that are superseded, broken, or no longer needed. 308 309 **[Jettison](#Jettison)** — Delete a specific image tag from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 310 [Jettisoning](#Jettison) is lower-level than [Abjure](#Abjure) — it removes a single tag rather than a complete [Hallmark](#Hallmark) artifact set. 311 Used for cleanup of individual registry entries. 312 313 ### Diagnostics 314 315 **[List Depots](#ListDepots)** — Inventory all active [Depots](#Depot) visible to the current [Payor](#Payor) credentials. 316 Shows project IDs, regions, and provisioning status. 317 318 **[JWT Probe](#JWTProbe)** — Test service account authentication. 319 The [JWT Probe](#JWTProbe) verifies that a [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) credential can successfully authenticate to the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project — useful for diagnosing access failures after credential creation or rotation. 320 321 **[OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe)** — Test [Payor](#Payor) OAuth authentication. 322 The [OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe) verifies that the stored refresh token can obtain a valid access token — useful for diagnosing [Payor](#Payor) operation failures before attempting a full [Refresh](#Refresh). 323 324 ## Appendix: Crucible Operations 325 326 Formal definitions for all [Crucible](#Crucible) operations. 327 328 ### Lifecycle 329 330 **[Charge](#Charge)** — Start the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 331 [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. 332 333 **[Quench](#Quench)** — Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Nameplate's](#Nameplate) containers. 334 [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). 335 336 ### Interaction 337 338 **[Rack](#Rack)** — Shell into a [Bottle](#Bottle) container. 339 [Racking](#Rack) opens an interactive session inside the running workload — for debugging, inspecting state, or running commands as the [Bottle](#Bottle) user would experience them. 340 341 **[Hail](#Hail)** — Shell into a [Sentry](#Sentry) container. 342 [Hailing](#Hail) opens an interactive session on the gateway — for inspecting `iptables` rules, `dnsmasq` configuration, network state, and egress logs. 343 344 **[Scry](#Scry)** — Observe network traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. 345 [Scrying](#Scry) captures packets on the [Crucible's](#Crucible) network interfaces — for verifying that blocked traffic is actually blocked, diagnosing connectivity issues, or watching the [Sentry's](#Sentry) filtering in action. 346 347 ## Appendix: Adversarial Test Method 348 349 The [Crucible's](#Crucible) containment is validated through coordinated escape testing using two components:

[Establishing](#Establish) walks the [Payor](#Payor) through project creation, API enablement, and consent screen configuration — the manual prerequisites before any automated operations can run. **[Install](#Install)** — Ingest OAuth client credentials from a downloaded JSON key file. [Installing](#Install) triggers a browser authorization flow and stores the resulting refresh token locally with restrictive permissions (`600`). This is a one-time [Payor](#Payor) operation — the refresh token persists until explicitly revoked. **[Levy](#Levy)** — Provision a new [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure. [Levying](#Levy) creates the GCP project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and build configuration. This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that binds [Regime](#Regime) configuration to real cloud resources. **[Unmake](#Unmake)** — Permanently destroy a [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure — project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and all contents. [Unmaking](#Unmake) is the reverse of [Levying](#Levy). This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation and is irreversible. **[Refresh](#Refresh)** — Refresh an expired [Payor](#Payor) OAuth token. OAuth tokens expire periodically; [Refreshing](#Refresh) re-authenticates via the stored refresh token without repeating the full [Install](#Install) flow. Run when [Payor](#Payor) operations fail with authentication errors. **[Quota](#Quota)** — Review Cloud Build capacity and usage. [Quota](#Quota) displays the current build minute allocation, consumption, and any throttling in effect for the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project. ### Credentials **[Mantle](#Mantle)** — Create or replace the [Governor](#Governor) service account for a [Depot](#Depot). [Knighting](#Knight) provisions a new credential scoped to build and publish access within a single [Depot](#Depot). **[Charter](#Charter)** — Create a [Retriever](#Retriever) service account. [Chartering](#Charter) provisions a new credential scoped to image pull access within a single [Depot](#Depot). **[Forfeit](#Forfeit)** — Revoke a service account credential. [Forfeiting](#Forfeit) deletes the service account and its key material from the [Depot](#Depot) — the credential becomes permanently unusable. This is a [Governor](#Governor) operation used when a credential is compromised, no longer needed, or being rotated. **[List Service Accounts](#ListSAs)** — Inventory all service accounts issued within a [Depot](#Depot). content.content: 347 ## Appendix: Adversarial Test Method 348 349 The [Crucible's](#Crucible) containment is validated through coordinated escape testing using two components: 350 351 - **[Ifrit](#Ifrit)** — Adversarial attack [Vessel](#Vessel) purpose-built to run inside a [Bottle](#Bottle), seeking escape. The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) carries scapy (arbitrary packet construction), strace (syscall boundary probing), and a minimal footprint — tools chosen to probe every surface the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment exposes. Named for the djinn imprisoned in a bottle. 352 - **[Theurge](#Theurge)** — Test orchestrator running on the host, outside the [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Theurge](#Theurge) [Charges](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) as its [Bottle](#Bottle), then dispatches curated, reproducible, version-controlled attack scripts targeting specific surfaces: DNS exfiltration, ICMP covert channels, cloud metadata probing, namespace breakout, and direct IP bypass attempts. Each attack runs inside the [Bottle](#Bottle) while the [Theurge](#Theurge) simultaneously observes the [Sentry's](#Sentry) network from outside — confirming that blocked traffic is actually blocked, not merely unrequested. 353 354 The escape tests were developed through adversarial Claude Code sessions with full visibility into the [Sentry's](#Sentry) source, configuration, and the [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) specification. 355 The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) [Vessel](#Vessel) is the delivery vehicle; the intelligence came from the authoring process. 356 Every test that passes is evidence the containment holds — not proof. 357 The test suite grows as new attack surfaces are identified. 358 359 ## Appendix: Roadmap 360 361 The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: 362 363 - **VPC Service Controls** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 364 365 - **Cosign container signing** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. 366 367 - **CDN-aware IP gating** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. 368 369 - **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. 370 371 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case. 372 373 ## Appendix: Reference Project 374 375 This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle). 376 The annotated tree below maps its files to the concepts defined above. 377 378 | Path | Description | 379 |------|-------------| 380 | `Project Root/` | | 381 | `├── CLAUDE.md` | [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) command reference, glossary, conventions | 382 | `├── tt/` | 136 [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) — `tt/rbw-` for all operations | 383 | `├── Tools/` | | 384 | `│ ├── buk/` | Bash Utility Kit — portable CLI infrastructure | 385 | `│ └── rbk/` | Recipe Bottle Kit — domain logic | 386 | `├── .buk/` | [BURC](#BURC) project structure [Regime](#Regime) | 387 | `├── .rbk/` | [Regime](#Regime) configuration root | 388 | `│ ��── rbrp.env` | [RBRP](#RBRP) — [Payor](#Payor) identity for this [Depot](#Depot) | 389 | `│ ├── rbrr.env` | [RBRR](#RBRR) — [Depot](#Depot) identity and build configuration | 390 | `│ ├── tadmor/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — adversarial testing [Crucible](#Crucible) | 391 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + [Ifrit](#Ifrit), restrictive allowlist | 392 | `│ ├── srjcl/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — Jupyter notebook [Crucible](#Crucible) | 393 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + Jupyter, academic-domain allowlist | 394 | `│ └── pluml/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — PlantUML diagram server [Crucible](#Crucible) | 395 | `│ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + PlantUML, no-egress allowlist | 396 | `└── rbev-vessels/` | [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions | 397 | ` ├── rbev-sentry-debian-slim/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle) image | 398 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | debian-slim + iptables + dnsmasq | 399 | ` │ ├── rbjs_sentry.sh` | [Sentry](#Sentry) runtime — policy engine | 400 | ` │ ├── rbjp_pentacle.sh` | [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runtime — namespace setup | 401 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | 402 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack binary | 403 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | Rust binary + scapy + strace | 404 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | 405 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-plantuml/` | [Bind](#Bind) — upstream image pinned by digest | 406 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Bind](#Bind) mode, digest reference | 407 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-anthropic-jupyter/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — Jupyter notebook server | 408 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | | 409 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | 410 | ` └── (4 additional test vessels)` | busybox variants for [Theurge](#Theurge) fixture coverage | 411 412 ## Appendix: Specific Regimes 413 414 **[BURC](#BURC)** — Project structure configuration, in the repo. 415 [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) directory, tools directory. 416 417 **[BURS](#BURS)** — Developer workstation configuration. 418 Not in git. 419 Log directory, station paths. 420 421 **[RBRR](#RBRR)** — [Depot](#Depot) identity and build configuration — populated during [Levy](#Levy), consumed by [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) operations. 422 423 **[RBRP](#RBRP)** — [Payor](#Payor) administrative identity — GCP project, billing account, OAuth client ID, operator email. 424 In the repo. 425 426 **[RBRO](#RBRO)** — [Payor](#Payor) OAuth credentials — client secret and refresh token. 427 Not in the repo. 428 429 **[RBRA](#RBRA)** — Role credentials resident on user workstation, enabling [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) operations. 430 One credential file per role assignments. 435 436 ## License 437 438 Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. 439 440 Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0. 441

A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. - **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. - **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case. ## Appendix: Reference Project This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle). The annotated tree below maps its files to the concepts defined above. | Path | Description | |------|-------------| | `Project Root/` | | | `├── CLAUDE.md` | [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) command reference, glossary, conventions | | `├── tt/` | 136 [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) — `tt/rbw-` for all operations | | `├── Tools/` | | | `│ ├── buk/` | Bash Utility Kit — portable CLI infrastructure | | `│ └── rbk/` | Recipe Bottle Kit — domain logic | | `├── .buk/` | [BURC](#BURC) project structure [Regime](#Regime) | | `├── .rbk/` | [Regime](#Regime) configuration root | | `│ ��── rbrp.env` | [RBRP](#RBRP) — [Payor](#Payor) identity for this [Depot](#Depot) | | `│ ├── rbrr.env` | [RBRR](#RBRR) — [Depot](#Depot) identity and build configuration | | `│ ├── tadmor/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — adversarial testing [Crucible](#Crucible) | | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + [Ifrit](#Ifrit), restrictive allowlist | | `│ ├── srjcl/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — Jupyter notebook [Crucible](#Crucible) | | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + Jupyter, academic-domain allowlist | | `│ └── pluml/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — PlantUML diagram server [Crucible](#Crucible) | | `│ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + PlantUML, no-egress allowlist | | `└── rbev-vessels/` | [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions | | ` ├── rbev-sentry-debian-slim/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle) image | | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | debian-slim + iptables + dnsmasq | | ` │ ├── rbjs_sentry.sh` | [Sentry](#Sentry) runtime — policy engine | | ` │ ├── rbjp_pentacle.sh` | [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runtime — namespace setup | | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack binary | | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | Rust binary + scapy + strace | | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | | ` ├── rbev-bottle-plantuml/` | [Bind](#Bind) — upstream image pinned by digest | | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Bind](#Bind) mode, digest reference | | ` ├── rbev-bottle-anthropic-jupyter/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — Jupyter notebook server | | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | | | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | | ` └── (4 additional test vessels)` | busybox variants for [Theurge](#Theurge) fixture coverage | ## Appendix: Specific Regimes **[BURC](#BURC)** — Project structure configuration, in the repo. [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) directory, tools directory. **[BURS](#BURS)** — Developer workstation configuration. Not in git. Log directory, station paths. **[RBRR](#RBRR)** — [Depot](#Depot) identity and build configuration — populated during [Levy](#Levy), consumed by [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) operations. **[RBRP](#RBRP)** — [Payor](#Payor) administrative identity — GCP project, billing account, OAuth client ID, operator email. In the repo. **[RBRO](#RBRO)** — [Payor](#Payor) OAuth credentials — client secret and refresh token. Not in the repo. **[RBRA](#RBRA)** — Role credentials resident on user workstation, enabling [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) operations. One credential file per role per [Depot](#Depot). **[RBRV](#RBRV)** — [Vessel](#Vessel) configuration specifying [Bind](#Bind), [Conjure](#Conjure), or [Graft](#Graft) mode for creating [Hallmarks](#Hallmark). **[RBRN](#RBRN)** — Per-[Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Crucible](#Crucible) configuration mapping two [Vessels](#Vessel) — [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) — with runtime and [Hallmark](#Hallmark) assignments. ## License Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0.

input.old_string:
### Crucible Lifecycle

[Charge](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) to start the [Sentry](#Sentry), [Pentacle](#Pentacle), and [Bottle](#Bottle) together — the [Bottle](#Bottle) is ready for interactive use immediately.
[Rack](#Rack) the [Bottle](#Bottle) to shell in, [Hail](#Hail) the [Sentry](#Sentry) to inspect the gateway, or [Scry](#Scry) the network to observe traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers.
When finished, [Quench](#Quench) the [Crucible](#Crucible) to stop and clean up all three containers.
To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its provenance — the full view shows the SBOM, build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain.

## Appendix: Foundry Operations
input.new_string: ### Crucible Lifecycle [Charge](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) to start the [Sentry](#Sentry), [Pentacle](#Pentacle), and [Bottle](#Bottle) together — the [Bottle](#Bottle) is ready for interactive use immediately. [Rack](#Rack) the [Bottle](#Bottle) to shell in, [Hail](#Hail) the [Sentry](#Sentry) to inspect the gateway, or [Scry](#Scry) the network to observe traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. When finished, [Quench](#Quench) the [Crucible](#Crucible) to stop and clean up all three containers. To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its provenance — the full view shows the SBOM, build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. ### Reference Nameplates Shipped [Nameplates](#Nameplate) demonstrating different [Crucible](#Crucible) configurations. Each pairs a [Sentry](#Sentry) with a [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) and defines the network policy for that deployment target. **[ccyolo](#ccyolo)** — Claude Code sandbox for network-contained AI development. The [ccyolo](#ccyolo) [Crucible](#Crucible) runs Claude Code inside a [Bottle](#Bottle) that can only reach Anthropic — SSH entry from the workstation, OAuth authentication via copy/paste, everything else blocked. [Kludge](#Kludge)-only development target: no cloud account, no service account credentials, fully self-contained on the developer's workstation. The onboarding handbook's first hands-on track teaches the full [Crucible](#Crucible) lifecycle using [ccyolo](#ccyolo). **[tadmor](#tadmor)** — Adversarial security testing [Crucible](#Crucible). The [tadmor](#tadmor) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack [Vessel](#Vessel) under a restrictive network allowlist. The [Theurge](#Theurge) test orchestrator [Charges](#Charge) [tadmor](#tadmor) and dispatches curated escape attempts to validate that the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment holds under adversarial conditions. ## Appendix: Foundry Operations
